revision.text:
{{Short description|Burial site in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Matsuoka Kofun | native_name = 松岡古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image = Tegurigajoyama Kofun.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Tegurigajoyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Matsuoka Kofun | relief = 1 | map_caption = Matsuoka Kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|36|05|35|N|136|18|53.2|E|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Eiheiji, Fukui]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Hokuriku region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th or early 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Special Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Matsuoka Kofun Cluster'''|松岡古墳群|Matsuoka Kofun-gun}} is a group ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]]s located in what is now part of the town of [[Eiheiji, Fukui]] in the [[Hokuriku region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1977 in the case of the Tegurigajoyama Kofun, the largest of the group. Protection was extended to the remainder of the group in July 2005.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210110 |title=松岡古墳群 手繰ヶ城山古墳 石舟山古墳 鳥越山古墳 二本松山古墳 |trans-title= Matsuoka kofun gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The ''kofun'' group is located on a hillside with an elevation of 160 meters, overlooking the [[Kuzuryū River]] and the Echizen Plain. The cluster consists of about 150 tumuli, which are a mixture of square tumuli, circular tumuli, and keyhole-shaped tumuli. Among these tumuli, the four large keyhole-shape tumuli, ({{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above), which are estimated to date from the mid 4th to the late 5th century AD are covered under the National Historic Site designation. The tombs do not appear in any historical records and the name of rank of the persons buried within are unknown, although per local legend, these were the graves of the "Kings of [[Koshi Province (Japan)|Koshi]]" (越の国), the pre-[[Imperial House of Japan|imperial dynasty]] state which controlled present-day Fukui, [[Ishikawa Prefecture|Ishikawa]], [[Toyama Prefecture|Toyama]] and [[Niigata Prefecture]]s. [[Emperor Keitai]] ((reigned 507 – 531 AD)) is said in some semi-legendary historical records to have originally been the King of Koshi, and therefore that the ''kofun'' in this area are the tombs for members of his clan.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
It is about a 20-minute walk from [[Shiizakai Station]] on the [[Echizen Railway]] [[Katsuyama Eiheiji Line]] to the Tegurigajoyama Kofun.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==The kofun== {{nihongo|'''Tegurigajoyama Kofun'''|手繰ヶ城山古墳|}} is the largest of the four tumuli, with a total length of 128.4 meters, making it is the second largest found in the Hokuriku region of Japan after the [[Rokuroseyama Kofun Cluster|Rokuroseiyama Kofun]]. The circular portion is 78 meters in diameter and 19.7 meters high; the rectangular portion is in two tiers, with a length of 50.4 meters, width of 56.5 meters and a height of 15.2 meters. The tumulus is orientated to the northeast. It has a peculiar morphology in which the front part is extremely widened like a drumstick. Large numbers of ''[[fukiishi]]'' and cylindrical ''[[haniwa]]'' were found in the area. The details of the interior are unknown as the ''kofun'' has not been [[archaeological excavation|excavated]]; however, [[ground-penetrating radar]] has confirmed that a stone-lined [[burial chamber]] exists near the apex of the posterior circle. The tumulus was dated to the middle of the fourth century AD.
{{nihongo|'''Torigoeyama Kofun'''|鳥越山古墳|}} is the smallest of the four tumuli, with a total length of 53.7 meters; however, it was found to have a boat-shaped stone [[sarcophagus]] and two horizontal passage graves. A large number of [[grave goods]] were also uncovered, including stone spearheads, [[Sharpening stone|whetstone]]s, fragments of iron implements, [[earthenware]] and horse fittings. The tumulus was partly carved out of the ground, and has no tiers. ''Haniwa'' have been found at the top of the posterior circle. It was dated to the middle of the fifth century.
{{nihongo|'''Ishifuneyama Kofun'''|石舟山古墳|}} is also a keyhole-shaped tumulus, and has a total length of 79.1 meters, with a secondary tumulus on its southwest side. The main tumulus has two-tiers, and ''haniwa'' have been found. It also dates from the middle of the fifth century. It was robbed at some unknown time, and fragments of a boat-shaped stone sarcophagus were exposed, thus leading to its name.
{{nihongo|'''Nihonmatsuyama Kofun'''|二本松山古墳|}} is a dome-shaped tumulus with a total length of 89 meters, although there is considerable controversy as to its structure, and prevalent option is that it is actually a [[Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped tumulus]] which has partly collapsed. Neither [[fukiishi" nor ''haniwa'' have been found. It is built facing southwest on the summit of Nihonmatsuyama at an elevation of 273 meters on the south bank of the point where the Kuzuryu River enters the Fukui Plain. It was excavated in the [[Edo period]] (1716–1736) at which time records indicate that armor and human remains were found and reburied. However, when was extensively excavated in the [[Meiji period]] only a number of grave goods were recovered, including [[bronze mirror]]s, fragments of a crown, cylindrical beads, swords and other fragments of weapons and a boat-shaped sarcophagus, but no armor or bones. The tumulus dates to the late fifth century. According to legend, this tumulus is the grave of Furuhime, the mother of the semi-legendary Emperor Keitai.
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukui)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:Matsuoka Kofun}} *[http://info.pref.fukui.jp/bunka/bunkazai/sitei/siseki/eiheijicho-matsuoka.html Cultural Heritage of Fukui Prefecture] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://kankou.town.eiheiji.lg.jp/guide/index.php?pathinfo=kofungun Eiheiji town guide] {{in lang|ja}}

Among these tumuli, the four large keyhole-shape tumuli, ({{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above), which are estimated to date from the mid 4th to the late 5th century AD are covered under the National Historic Site designation. The tombs do not appear in any historical records and the name of Rank of the persons buried within are unknown, although per local legend, these were the graves of the "Kings of [[:en:Koshi Province (Japan)|Koshi]]" (越の国), the pre-[[:en:Imperial House of Japan|imperial dynasty]] state which controlled present-day Fukui, [[:en:Ishikawa Prefecture|Ishikawa]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama]] and [[:en:Niigata Prefecture|Niigata Prefecture]]s. [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] ((reigned 507 – 531 AD)) is said in some semi-legendary historical records to have originally been the King of Koshi, and therefore that the ''kofun'' in this area are the tombs for members of his clan.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
It is about a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Shiizakai Station|Shiizakai Station]] on the [[:en:Echizen Railway|Echizen Railway]] [[:en:Katsuyama Eiheiji Line|Katsuyama Eiheiji Line]] to the Tegurigajoyama Kofun.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==The kofun== {{nihongo|'''Tegurigajoyama Kofun'''|手繰ヶ城山古墳|}} is the largest of the four tumuli, with a total length of 128.4 meters, making it is the second largest found in the Hokuriku region of Japan after the [[Rokuroseyama Kofun Cluster|Rokuroseiyama Kofun]]. The circular portion is 78 meters in diameter and 19.7 meters high; the rectangular portion is in two tiers, with a length of 50.4 meters, width of 56.5 meters and a height of 15.2 meters. The tumulus is orientated to the northeast. It has a peculiar morphology in which the front part is extremely widened like a drumstick. Large numbers of ''[[fukiishi]]'' and cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' were found in the area. The details of the interior are unknown as the ''kofun'' has not been [[archaeological excavation|excavated]]; however, [[:en:Ground-penetrating radar|Ground-penetrating radar]] has confirmed that a stone-lined [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] exists near the apex of the posterior circle. The tumulus was dated to the middle of the fourth century AD.
{{nihongo|'''Torigoeyama Kofun'''|鳥越山古墳|}} is the smallest of the four tumuli, with a total length of 53.7 meters; however, it was found to have a boat-shaped stone [[:en:Sarcophagus|Sarcophagus]] and two horizontal passage graves. A large number of [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] were also uncovered, including stone spearheads, [[:en:Sharpening stone|whetstone]]s, fragments of iron implements, [[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] and horse fittings. The tumulus was partly carved out of the ground, and has no tiers. ''Haniwa'' have been found at the top of the posterior circle. It was dated to the middle of the fifth century.
{{nihongo|'''Ishifuneyama Kofun'''|石舟山古墳|}} is also a keyhole-shaped tumulus, and has a total length of 79.1 meters, with a secondary tumulus on its southwest side. The main tumulus has two-tiers, and ''haniwa'' have been found. It also dates from the middle of the fifth century. It was robbed at some unknown time, and fragments of a boat-shaped stone sarcophagus were exposed, thus leading to its name.
{{nihongo|'''Nihonmatsuyama Kofun'''|二本松山古墳|}} is a dome-shaped tumulus with a total length of 89 meters, although there is considerable controversy as to its structure, and prevalent option is that it is actually a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped tumulus]] which has partly collapsed. Neither [[fukiishi" nor ''haniwa'' have been found. It is built facing southwest on the summit of Nihonmatsuyama at an elevation of 273 meters on the south bank of the point where the Kuzuryu River enters the Fukui Plain. It was excavated in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1716–1736) at which time records indicate that armor and human remains were found and reburied. However, when was extensively excavated in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] only a number of grave goods were recovered, including [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s, fragments of a crown, cylindrical beads, swords and other fragments of weapons and a boat-shaped sarcophagus, but no armor or bones. The tumulus dates to the late fifth century. According to legend, this tumulus is the grave of Furuhime, the mother of the semi-legendary Emperor Keitai.
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukui)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukui)]]

The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1977 in the case of the Tegurigajoyama Kofun, the largest of the group. Protection was extended to the remainder of the group in July 2005.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210110 |title=松岡古墳群 手繰ヶ城山古墳 石舟山古墳 鳥越山古墳 二本松山古墳 |trans-title= Matsuoka kofun gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>

The tumulus was dated to the middle of the fourth century AD.
{{nihongo|'''Torigoeyama Kofun'''|鳥越山古墳|}} is the smallest of the four tumuli, with a total length of 53.7 meters; however, it was found to have a boat-shaped stone [[:en:Sarcophagus|Sarcophagus]] and two horizontal passage graves. revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Matsuoka Kofun]][[en:Matsuoka Kofun Cluster]][[ja:松岡古墳群]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17993485]] Wikidata name: Matsuoka Kofun Cluster [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Burial site in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Matsuoka Kofun | native_name = 松岡古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image =Tegurigajoyama Kofun.jpg; File:Tegurigajoyama Kofun.jpg| image_size = | alt = | caption = Tegurigajoyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Matsuoka Kofun | relief = 1 | map_caption = Matsuoka Kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates ={{coord|36|05|35|N|136|18|53.2|E|display=inline}}; {{Coord|36.093056|N|136.314778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}| map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Eiheiji, Fukui|Eiheiji, Fukui]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th or early 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Special Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Matsuoka Kofun Cluster'''|松岡古墳群|Matsuoka Kofun-gun}} is a group ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]]s located in what is now part of the town of [[:en:Eiheiji, Fukui|Eiheiji, Fukui]] in the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1977 in the case of the Tegurigajoyama Kofun, the largest of the group. Protection was extended to the remainder of the group in July 2005.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210110 |title=松岡古墳群 手繰ヶ城山古墳 石舟山古墳 鳥越山古墳 二本松山古墳 |trans-title= Matsuoka kofun gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The ''kofun'' group is located on a hillside with an elevation of 160 meters, overlooking the [[:en:Kuzuryū River|Kuzuryū River]] and the Echizen Plain. The cluster consists of about 150 tumuli, which are a mixture of square tumuli, circular tumuli, and keyhole-shaped tumuli. Among these tumuli, the four large keyhole-shape tumuli, ({{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above), which are estimated to date from the mid 4th to the late 5th century AD are covered under the National Historic Site designation. The tombs do not appear in any historical records and the name of Rank of the persons buried within are unknown, although per local legend, these were the graves of the "Kings of [[:en:Koshi Province (Japan)|Koshi]]" (越の国), the pre-[[:en:Imperial House of Japan|imperial dynasty]] state which controlled present-day Fukui, [[:en:Ishikawa Prefecture|Ishikawa]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama]] and [[:en:Niigata Prefecture|Niigata Prefecture]]s. [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] ((reigned 507 – 531 AD)) is said in some semi-legendary historical records to have originally been the King of Koshi, and therefore that the ''kofun'' in this area are the tombs for members of his clan.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
It is about a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Shiizakai Station|Shiizakai Station]] on the [[:en:Echizen Railway|Echizen Railway]] [[:en:Katsuyama Eiheiji Line|Katsuyama Eiheiji Line]] to the Tegurigajoyama Kofun.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==The kofun== {{nihongo|'''Tegurigajoyama Kofun'''|手繰ヶ城山古墳|}} is the largest of the four tumuli, with a total length of 128.4 meters, making it is the second largest found in the Hokuriku region of Japan after the [[Rokuroseyama Kofun Cluster|Rokuroseiyama Kofun]]. The circular portion is 78 meters in diameter and 19.7 meters high; the rectangular portion is in two tiers, with a length of 50.4 meters, width of 56.5 meters and a height of 15.2 meters. The tumulus is orientated to the northeast. It has a peculiar morphology in which the front part is extremely widened like a drumstick. Large numbers of ''[[fukiishi]]'' and cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' were found in the area. The details of the interior are unknown as the ''kofun'' has not been [[archaeological excavation|excavated]]; however, [[:en:Ground-penetrating radar|Ground-penetrating radar]] has confirmed that a stone-lined [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] exists near the apex of the posterior circle. The tumulus was dated to the middle of the fourth century AD.
{{nihongo|'''Torigoeyama Kofun'''|鳥越山古墳|}} is the smallest of the four tumuli, with a total length of 53.7 meters; however, it was found to have a boat-shaped stone [[:en:Sarcophagus|Sarcophagus]] and two horizontal passage graves. A large number of [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] were also uncovered, including stone spearheads, [[:en:Sharpening stone|whetstone]]s, fragments of iron implements, [[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] and horse fittings. The tumulus was partly carved out of the ground, and has no tiers. ''Haniwa'' have been found at the top of the posterior circle. It was dated to the middle of the fifth century.
{{nihongo|'''Ishifuneyama Kofun'''|石舟山古墳|}} is also a keyhole-shaped tumulus, and has a total length of 79.1 meters, with a secondary tumulus on its southwest side. The main tumulus has two-tiers, and ''haniwa'' have been found. It also dates from the middle of the fifth century. It was robbed at some unknown time, and fragments of a boat-shaped stone sarcophagus were exposed, thus leading to its name.
{{nihongo|'''Nihonmatsuyama Kofun'''|二本松山古墳|}} is a dome-shaped tumulus with a total length of 89 meters, although there is considerable controversy as to its structure, and prevalent option is that it is actually a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped tumulus]] which has partly collapsed. Neither [[fukiishi" nor ''haniwa'' have been found. It is built facing southwest on the summit of Nihonmatsuyama at an elevation of 273 meters on the south bank of the point where the Kuzuryu River enters the Fukui Plain. It was excavated in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1716–1736) at which time records indicate that armor and human remains were found and reburied. However, when was extensively excavated in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] only a number of grave goods were recovered, including [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s, fragments of a crown, cylindrical beads, swords and other fragments of weapons and a boat-shaped sarcophagus, but no armor or bones. The tumulus dates to the late fifth century. According to legend, this tumulus is the grave of Furuhime, the mother of the semi-legendary Emperor Keitai.
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukui)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukui)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:Matsuoka Kofun}} *[http://info.pref.fukui.jp/bunka/bunkazai/sitei/siseki/eiheijicho-matsuoka.html Cultural Heritage of Fukui Prefecture] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://kankou.town.eiheiji.lg.jp/guide/index.php?pathinfo=kofungun Eiheiji town guide] {{in lang|ja}}

However, when was extensively excavated in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] only a number of grave goods were recovered, including [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s, fragments of a crown, cylindrical beads, swords and other fragments of weapons and a boat-shaped sarcophagus, but no armor or bones. The tumulus dates to the late fifth century. According to legend, this tumulus is the grave of Furuhime, the mother of the semi-legendary Emperor Keitai.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Matsuoka Kofun]][[en:Matsuoka Kofun Cluster]][[ja:松岡古墳群]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17993485]] Wikidata name: Matsuoka Kofun Cluster [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Burial site in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Matsuoka Kofun | native_name = 松岡古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image =Tegurigajoyama Kofun.jpg; File:Tegurigajoyama Kofun.jpg| image_size = | alt = | caption = Tegurigajoyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Matsuoka Kofun | relief = 1 | map_caption = Matsuoka Kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates ={{coord|36|05|35|N|136|18|53.2|E|display=inline}}; {{Coord|36.093056|N|136.314778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}| map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Eiheiji, Fukui|Eiheiji, Fukui]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th or early 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Special Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Matsuoka Kofun Cluster'''|松岡古墳群|Matsuoka Kofun-gun}} is a group ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]]s located in what is now part of the town of [[:en:Eiheiji, Fukui|Eiheiji, Fukui]] in the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1977 in the case of the Tegurigajoyama Kofun, the largest of the group. Protection was extended to the remainder of the group in July 2005.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210110 |title=松岡古墳群 手繰ヶ城山古墳 石舟山古墳 鳥越山古墳 二本松山古墳 |trans-title= Matsuoka kofun gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The ''kofun'' group is located on a hillside with an elevation of 160 meters, overlooking the [[:en:Kuzuryū River|Kuzuryū River]] and the Echizen Plain. The cluster consists of about 150 tumuli, which are a mixture of square tumuli, circular tumuli, and keyhole-shaped tumuli. Among these tumuli, the four large keyhole-shape tumuli, ({{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above), which are estimated to date from the mid 4th to the late 5th century AD are covered under the National Historic Site designation. The tombs do not appear in any historical records and the name of Rank of the persons buried within are unknown, although per local legend, these were the graves of the "Kings of [[:en:Koshi Province (Japan)|Koshi]]" (越の国), the pre-[[:en:Imperial House of Japan|imperial dynasty]] state which controlled present-day Fukui, [[:en:Ishikawa Prefecture|Ishikawa]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama]] and [[:en:Niigata Prefecture|Niigata Prefecture]]s. [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] ((reigned 507 – 531 AD)) is said in some semi-legendary historical records to have originally been the King of Koshi, and therefore that the ''kofun'' in this area are the tombs for members of his clan.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
It is about a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Shiizakai Station|Shiizakai Station]] on the [[:en:Echizen Railway|Echizen Railway]] [[:en:Katsuyama Eiheiji Line|Katsuyama Eiheiji Line]] to the Tegurigajoyama Kofun.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==The kofun== {{nihongo|'''Tegurigajoyama Kofun'''|手繰ヶ城山古墳|}} is the largest of the four tumuli, with a total length of 128.4 meters, making it is the second largest found in the Hokuriku region of Japan after the [[Rokuroseyama Kofun Cluster|Rokuroseiyama Kofun]]. The circular portion is 78 meters in diameter and 19.7 meters high; the rectangular portion is in two tiers, with a length of 50.4 meters, width of 56.5 meters and a height of 15.2 meters. The tumulus is orientated to the northeast. It has a peculiar morphology in which the front part is extremely widened like a drumstick. Large numbers of ''[[fukiishi]]'' and cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' were found in the area. The details of the interior are unknown as the ''kofun'' has not been [[archaeological excavation|excavated]]; however, [[:en:Ground-penetrating radar|Ground-penetrating radar]] has confirmed that a stone-lined [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] exists near the apex of the posterior circle. The tumulus was dated to the middle of the fourth century AD.
{{nihongo|'''Torigoeyama Kofun'''|鳥越山古墳|}} is the smallest of the four tumuli, with a total length of 53.7 meters; however, it was found to have a boat-shaped stone [[:en:Sarcophagus|Sarcophagus]] and two horizontal passage graves. A large number of [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] were also uncovered, including stone spearheads, [[:en:Sharpening stone|whetstone]]s, fragments of iron implements, [[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] and horse fittings. The tumulus was partly carved out of the ground, and has no tiers. ''Haniwa'' have been found at the top of the posterior circle. It was dated to the middle of the fifth century.
{{nihongo|'''Ishifuneyama Kofun'''|石舟山古墳|}} is also a keyhole-shaped tumulus, and has a total length of 79.1 meters, with a secondary tumulus on its southwest side. The main tumulus has two-tiers, and ''haniwa'' have been found. It also dates from the middle of the fifth century. It was robbed at some unknown time, and fragments of a boat-shaped stone sarcophagus were exposed, thus leading to its name.
{{nihongo|'''Nihonmatsuyama Kofun'''|二本松山古墳|}} is a dome-shaped tumulus with a total length of 89 meters, although there is considerable controversy as to its structure, and prevalent option is that it is actually a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped tumulus]] which has partly collapsed. Neither [[fukiishi" nor ''haniwa'' have been found. It is built facing southwest on the summit of Nihonmatsuyama at an elevation of 273 meters on the south bank of the point where the Kuzuryu River enters the Fukui Plain. It was excavated in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1716–1736) at which time records indicate that armor and human remains were found and reburied. However, when was extensively excavated in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] only a number of grave goods were recovered, including [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s, fragments of a crown, cylindrical beads, swords and other fragments of weapons and a boat-shaped sarcophagus, but no armor or bones. The tumulus dates to the late fifth century. According to legend, this tumulus is the grave of Furuhime, the mother of the semi-legendary Emperor Keitai.

The details of the interior are unknown as the ''kofun'' has not been [[archaeological excavation|excavated]]; however, [[:en:Ground-penetrating radar|Ground-penetrating radar]] has confirmed that a stone-lined [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] exists near the apex of the posterior circle. The tumulus was dated to the middle of the fourth century AD.
{{nihongo|'''Torigoeyama Kofun'''|鳥越山古墳|}} is the smallest of the four tumuli, with a total length of 53.7 meters; however, it was found to have a boat-shaped stone [[:en:Sarcophagus|Sarcophagus]] and two horizontal passage graves. A large number of [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] were also uncovered, including stone spearheads, [[:en:Sharpening stone|whetstone]]s, fragments of iron implements, [[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] and horse fittings. The tumulus was partly carved out of the ground, and has no tiers. ''Haniwa'' have been found at the top of the posterior circle. It was dated to the middle of the fifth century.
{{nihongo|'''Ishifuneyama Kofun'''|石舟山古墳|}} is also a keyhole-shaped tumulus, and has a total length of 79.1 meters, with a secondary tumulus on its southwest side. The main tumulus has two-tiers, and ''haniwa'' have been found. It also dates from the middle of the fifth century. It was robbed at some unknown time, and fragments of a boat-shaped stone sarcophagus were exposed, thus leading to its name.
{{nihongo|'''Nihonmatsuyama Kofun'''|二本松山古墳|}} is a dome-shaped tumulus with a total length of 89 meters, although there is considerable controversy as to its structure, and prevalent option is that it is actually a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped tumulus]] which has partly collapsed. Neither [[fukiishi" nor ''haniwa'' have been found. It is built facing southwest on the summit of Nihonmatsuyama at an elevation of 273 meters on the south bank of the point where the Kuzuryu River enters the Fukui Plain. It was excavated in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1716–1736) at which time records indicate that armor and human remains were found and reburied. However, when was extensively excavated in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] only a number of grave goods were recovered, including [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s, fragments of a crown, cylindrical beads, swords and other fragments of weapons and a boat-shaped sarcophagus, but no armor or bones. The tumulus dates to the late fifth century. According to legend, this tumulus is the grave of Furuhime, the mother of the semi-legendary Emperor Keitai.
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukui)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukui)]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Matsuoka Kofun]][[en:Matsuoka Kofun Cluster]][[ja:松岡古墳群]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17993485]] Wikidata name: Matsuoka Kofun Cluster [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Burial site in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Matsuoka Kofun | native_name = 松岡古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image =Tegurigajoyama Kofun.jpg; File:Tegurigajoyama Kofun.jpg| image_size = | alt = | caption = Tegurigajoyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Matsuoka Kofun | relief = 1 | map_caption = Matsuoka Kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates ={{coord|36|05|35|N|136|18|53.2|E|display=inline}}; {{Coord|36.093056|N|136.314778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}| map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Eiheiji, Fukui|Eiheiji, Fukui]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th or early 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Special Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Matsuoka Kofun Cluster'''|松岡古墳群|Matsuoka Kofun-gun}} is a group ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]]s located in what is now part of the town of [[:en:Eiheiji, Fukui|Eiheiji, Fukui]] in the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1977 in the case of the Tegurigajoyama Kofun, the largest of the group. Protection was extended to the remainder of the group in July 2005.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210110 |title=松岡古墳群 手繰ヶ城山古墳 石舟山古墳 鳥越山古墳 二本松山古墳 |trans-title= Matsuoka kofun gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The ''kofun'' group is located on a hillside with an elevation of 160 meters, overlooking the [[:en:Kuzuryū River|Kuzuryū River]] and the Echizen Plain. The cluster consists of about 150 tumuli, which are a mixture of square tumuli, circular tumuli, and keyhole-shaped tumuli. Among these tumuli, the four large keyhole-shape tumuli, ({{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above), which are estimated to date from the mid 4th to the late 5th century AD are covered under the National Historic Site designation. The tombs do not appear in any historical records and the name of Rank of the persons buried within are unknown, although per local legend, these were the graves of the "Kings of [[:en:Koshi Province (Japan)|Koshi]]" (越の国), the pre-[[:en:Imperial House of Japan|imperial dynasty]] state which controlled present-day Fukui, [[:en:Ishikawa Prefecture|Ishikawa]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama]] and [[:en:Niigata Prefecture|Niigata Prefecture]]s. [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] ((reigned 507 – 531 AD)) is said in some semi-legendary historical records to have originally been the King of Koshi, and therefore that the ''kofun'' in this area are the tombs for members of his clan.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
It is about a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Shiizakai Station|Shiizakai Station]] on the [[:en:Echizen Railway|Echizen Railway]] [[:en:Katsuyama Eiheiji Line|Katsuyama Eiheiji Line]] to the Tegurigajoyama Kofun.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==The kofun== {{nihongo|'''Tegurigajoyama Kofun'''|手繰ヶ城山古墳|}} is the largest of the four tumuli, with a total length of 128.4 meters, making it is the second largest found in the Hokuriku region of Japan after the [[Rokuroseyama Kofun Cluster|Rokuroseiyama Kofun]]. The circular portion is 78 meters in diameter and 19.7 meters high; the rectangular portion is in two tiers, with a length of 50.4 meters, width of 56.5 meters and a height of 15.2 meters. The tumulus is orientated to the northeast. It has a peculiar morphology in which the front part is extremely widened like a drumstick. Large numbers of ''[[fukiishi]]'' and cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' were found in the area. The details of the interior are unknown as the ''kofun'' has not been [[archaeological excavation|excavated]]; however, [[:en:Ground-penetrating radar|Ground-penetrating radar]] has confirmed that a stone-lined [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] exists near the apex of the posterior circle. The tumulus was dated to the middle of the fourth century AD.
{{nihongo|'''Torigoeyama Kofun'''|鳥越山古墳|}} is the smallest of the four tumuli, with a total length of 53.7 meters; however, it was found to have a boat-shaped stone [[:en:Sarcophagus|Sarcophagus]] and two horizontal passage graves. A large number of [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] were also uncovered, including stone spearheads, [[:en:Sharpening stone|whetstone]]s, fragments of iron implements, [[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] and horse fittings. The tumulus was partly carved out of the ground, and has no tiers. ''Haniwa'' have been found at the top of the posterior circle. It was dated to the middle of the fifth century.
{{nihongo|'''Ishifuneyama Kofun'''|石舟山古墳|}} is also a keyhole-shaped tumulus, and has a total length of 79.1 meters, with a secondary tumulus on its southwest side. The main tumulus has two-tiers, and ''haniwa'' have been found. It also dates from the middle of the fifth century. It was robbed at some unknown time, and fragments of a boat-shaped stone sarcophagus were exposed, thus leading to its name.
{{nihongo|'''Nihonmatsuyama Kofun'''|二本松山古墳|}} is a dome-shaped tumulus with a total length of 89 meters, although there is considerable controversy as to its structure, and prevalent option is that it is actually a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped tumulus]] which has partly collapsed. Neither [[fukiishi" nor ''haniwa'' have been found. It is built facing southwest on the summit of Nihonmatsuyama at an elevation of 273 meters on the south bank of the point where the Kuzuryu River enters the Fukui Plain. It was excavated in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1716–1736) at which time records indicate that armor and human remains were found and reburied. However, when was extensively excavated in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] only a number of grave goods were recovered, including [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s, fragments of a crown, cylindrical beads, swords and other fragments of weapons and a boat-shaped sarcophagus, but no armor or bones. The tumulus dates to the late fifth century. According to legend, this tumulus is the grave of Furuhime, the mother of the semi-legendary Emperor Keitai.

The tombs do not appear in any historical records and the name of Rank of the persons buried within are unknown, although per local legend, these were the graves of the "Kings of [[:en:Koshi Province (Japan)|Koshi]]" (越の国), the pre-[[:en:Imperial House of Japan|imperial dynasty]] state which controlled present-day Fukui, [[:en:Ishikawa Prefecture|Ishikawa]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama]] and [[:en:Niigata Prefecture|Niigata Prefecture]]s. [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] ((reigned 507 – 531 AD)) is said in some semi-legendary historical records to have originally been the King of Koshi, and therefore that the ''kofun'' in this area are the tombs for members of his clan.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
It is about a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Shiizakai Station|Shiizakai Station]] on the [[:en:Echizen Railway|Echizen Railway]] [[:en:Katsuyama Eiheiji Line|Katsuyama Eiheiji Line]] to the Tegurigajoyama Kofun.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==The kofun== {{nihongo|'''Tegurigajoyama Kofun'''|手繰ヶ城山古墳|}} is the largest of the four tumuli, with a total length of 128.4 meters, making it is the second largest found in the Hokuriku region of Japan after the [[Rokuroseyama Kofun Cluster|Rokuroseiyama Kofun]]. The circular portion is 78 meters in diameter and 19.7 meters high; the rectangular portion is in two tiers, with a length of 50.4 meters, width of 56.5 meters and a height of 15.2 meters. The tumulus is orientated to the northeast. It has a peculiar morphology in which the front part is extremely widened like a drumstick. Large numbers of ''[[fukiishi]]'' and cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' were found in the area. The details of the interior are unknown as the ''kofun'' has not been [[archaeological excavation|excavated]]; however, [[:en:Ground-penetrating radar|Ground-penetrating radar]] has confirmed that a stone-lined [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] exists near the apex of the posterior circle. The tumulus was dated to the middle of the fourth century AD.
{{nihongo|'''Torigoeyama Kofun'''|鳥越山古墳|}} is the smallest of the four tumuli, with a total length of 53.7 meters; however, it was found to have a boat-shaped stone [[:en:Sarcophagus|Sarcophagus]] and two horizontal passage graves. A large number of [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] were also uncovered, including stone spearheads, [[:en:Sharpening stone|whetstone]]s, fragments of iron implements, [[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] and horse fittings. The tumulus was partly carved out of the ground, and has no tiers. ''Haniwa'' have been found at the top of the posterior circle. It was dated to the middle of the fifth century.
{{nihongo|'''Ishifuneyama Kofun'''|石舟山古墳|}} is also a keyhole-shaped tumulus, and has a total length of 79.1 meters, with a secondary tumulus on its southwest side. The main tumulus has two-tiers, and ''haniwa'' have been found. It also dates from the middle of the fifth century. It was robbed at some unknown time, and fragments of a boat-shaped stone sarcophagus were exposed, thus leading to its name.
{{nihongo|'''Nihonmatsuyama Kofun'''|二本松山古墳|}} is a dome-shaped tumulus with a total length of 89 meters, although there is considerable controversy as to its structure, and prevalent option is that it is actually a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped tumulus]] which has partly collapsed. Neither [[fukiishi" nor ''haniwa'' have been found. It is built facing southwest on the summit of Nihonmatsuyama at an elevation of 273 meters on the south bank of the point where the Kuzuryu River enters the Fukui Plain. It was excavated in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1716–1736) at which time records indicate that armor and human remains were found and reburied. However, when was extensively excavated in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] only a number of grave goods were recovered, including [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s, fragments of a crown, cylindrical beads, swords and other fragments of weapons and a boat-shaped sarcophagus, but no armor or bones. The tumulus dates to the late fifth century. According to legend, this tumulus is the grave of Furuhime, the mother of the semi-legendary Emperor Keitai.
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukui)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukui)]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Matsuoka Kofun]][[en:Matsuoka Kofun Cluster]][[ja:松岡古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Matsuoka Kofun]][[en:Matsuoka Kofun Cluster]][[ja:松岡古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Matsuoka Kofun]][[en:Matsuoka Kofun Cluster]][[ja:松岡古墳群]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17993485]] Wikidata name: Matsuoka Kofun Cluster [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Burial site in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Matsuoka Kofun | native_name = 松岡古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image =Tegurigajoyama Kofun.jpg; File:Tegurigajoyama Kofun.jpg| image_size = | alt = | caption = Tegurigajoyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Matsuoka Kofun | relief = 1 | map_caption = Matsuoka Kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates ={{coord|36|05|35|N|136|18|53.2|E|display=inline}}; {{Coord|36.093056|N|136.314778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}| map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Eiheiji, Fukui|Eiheiji, Fukui]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th or early 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Special Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Matsuoka Kofun Cluster'''|松岡古墳群|Matsuoka Kofun-gun}} is a group ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]]s located in what is now part of the town of [[:en:Eiheiji, Fukui|Eiheiji, Fukui]] in the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1977 in the case of the Tegurigajoyama Kofun, the largest of the group. Protection was extended to the remainder of the group in July 2005.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210110 |title=松岡古墳群 手繰ヶ城山古墳 石舟山古墳 鳥越山古墳 二本松山古墳 |trans-title= Matsuoka kofun gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The ''kofun'' group is located on a hillside with an elevation of 160 meters, overlooking the [[:en:Kuzuryū River|Kuzuryū River]] and the Echizen Plain. The cluster consists of about 150 tumuli, which are a mixture of square tumuli, circular tumuli, and keyhole-shaped tumuli. Among these tumuli, the four large keyhole-shape tumuli, ({{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above), which are estimated to date from the mid 4th to the late 5th century AD are covered under the National Historic Site designation. The tombs do not appear in any historical records and the name of Rank of the persons buried within are unknown, although per local legend, these were the graves of the "Kings of [[:en:Koshi Province (Japan)|Koshi]]" (越の国), the pre-[[:en:Imperial House of Japan|imperial dynasty]] state which controlled present-day Fukui, [[:en:Ishikawa Prefecture|Ishikawa]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama]] and [[:en:Niigata Prefecture|Niigata Prefecture]]s. [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] ((reigned 507 – 531 AD)) is said in some semi-legendary historical records to have originally been the King of Koshi, and therefore that the ''kofun'' in this area are the tombs for members of his clan.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
It is about a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Shiizakai Station|Shiizakai Station]] on the [[:en:Echizen Railway|Echizen Railway]] [[:en:Katsuyama Eiheiji Line|Katsuyama Eiheiji Line]] to the Tegurigajoyama Kofun.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==The kofun== {{nihongo|'''Tegurigajoyama Kofun'''|手繰ヶ城山古墳|}} is the largest of the four tumuli, with a total length of 128.4 meters, making it is the second largest found in the Hokuriku region of Japan after the [[Rokuroseyama Kofun Cluster|Rokuroseiyama Kofun]]. The circular portion is 78 meters in diameter and 19.7 meters high; the rectangular portion is in two tiers, with a length of 50.4 meters, width of 56.5 meters and a height of 15.2 meters. The tumulus is orientated to the northeast. It has a peculiar morphology in which the front part is extremely widened like a drumstick. Large numbers of ''[[fukiishi]]'' and cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' were found in the area. The details of the interior are unknown as the ''kofun'' has not been [[archaeological excavation|excavated]]; however, [[:en:Ground-penetrating radar|Ground-penetrating radar]] has confirmed that a stone-lined [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] exists near the apex of the posterior circle. The tumulus was dated to the middle of the fourth century AD.
{{nihongo|'''Torigoeyama Kofun'''|鳥越山古墳|}} is the smallest of the four tumuli, with a total length of 53.7 meters; however, it was found to have a boat-shaped stone [[:en:Sarcophagus|Sarcophagus]] and two horizontal passage graves. A large number of [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] were also uncovered, including stone spearheads, [[:en:Sharpening stone|whetstone]]s, fragments of iron implements, [[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] and horse fittings. The tumulus was partly carved out of the ground, and has no tiers. ''Haniwa'' have been found at the top of the posterior circle. It was dated to the middle of the fifth century.
{{nihongo|'''Ishifuneyama Kofun'''|石舟山古墳|}} is also a keyhole-shaped tumulus, and has a total length of 79.1 meters, with a secondary tumulus on its southwest side. The main tumulus has two-tiers, and ''haniwa'' have been found. It also dates from the middle of the fifth century. It was robbed at some unknown time, and fragments of a boat-shaped stone sarcophagus were exposed, thus leading to its name.
{{nihongo|'''Nihonmatsuyama Kofun'''|二本松山古墳|}} is a dome-shaped tumulus with a total length of 89 meters, although there is considerable controversy as to its structure, and prevalent option is that it is actually a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped tumulus]] which has partly collapsed. Neither [[fukiishi" nor ''haniwa'' have been found. It is built facing southwest on the summit of Nihonmatsuyama at an elevation of 273 meters on the south bank of the point where the Kuzuryu River enters the Fukui Plain. It was excavated in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1716–1736) at which time records indicate that armor and human remains were found and reburied. However, when was extensively excavated in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] only a number of grave goods were recovered, including [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s, fragments of a crown, cylindrical beads, swords and other fragments of weapons and a boat-shaped sarcophagus, but no armor or bones. The tumulus dates to the late fifth century. According to legend, this tumulus is the grave of Furuhime, the mother of the semi-legendary Emperor Keitai.
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukui)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukui)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:Matsuoka Kofun}} *[http://info.pref.fukui.jp/bunka/bunkazai/sitei/siseki/eiheijicho-matsuoka.html Cultural Heritage of Fukui Prefecture] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://kankou.town.eiheiji.lg.jp/guide/index.php?pathinfo=kofungun Eiheiji town guide] {{in lang|ja}}

The main tumulus has two-tiers, and ''haniwa'' have been found. It also dates from the middle of the fifth century. It was robbed at some unknown time, and fragments of a boat-shaped stone sarcophagus were exposed, thus leading to its name.
{{nihongo|'''Nihonmatsuyama Kofun'''|二本松山古墳|}} is a dome-shaped tumulus with a total length of 89 meters, although there is considerable controversy as to its structure, and prevalent option is that it is actually a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped tumulus]] which has partly collapsed. Neither [[fukiishi" nor ''haniwa'' have been found. It is built facing southwest on the summit of Nihonmatsuyama at an elevation of 273 meters on the south bank of the point where the Kuzuryu River enters the Fukui Plain. It was excavated in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1716–1736) at which time records indicate that armor and human remains were found and reburied. However, when was extensively excavated in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] only a number of grave goods were recovered, including [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s, fragments of a crown, cylindrical beads, swords and other fragments of weapons and a boat-shaped sarcophagus, but no armor or bones. The tumulus dates to the late fifth century. According to legend, this tumulus is the grave of Furuhime, the mother of the semi-legendary Emperor Keitai.
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukui)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukui)]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Matsuoka Kofun]][[en:Matsuoka Kofun Cluster]][[ja:松岡古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Matsuoka Kofun]][[en:Matsuoka Kofun Cluster]][[ja:松岡古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Matsuoka Kofun]][[en:Matsuoka Kofun Cluster]][[ja:松岡古墳群]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17993485]] Wikidata name: Matsuoka Kofun Cluster [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Burial site in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Matsuoka Kofun | native_name = 松岡古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image =Tegurigajoyama Kofun.jpg; File:Tegurigajoyama Kofun.jpg| image_size = | alt = | caption = Tegurigajoyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Matsuoka Kofun | relief = 1 | map_caption = Matsuoka Kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates ={{coord|36|05|35|N|136|18|53.2|E|display=inline}}; {{Coord|36.093056|N|136.314778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}| map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Eiheiji, Fukui|Eiheiji, Fukui]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th or early 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Special Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Matsuoka Kofun Cluster'''|松岡古墳群|Matsuoka Kofun-gun}} is a group ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]]s located in what is now part of the town of [[:en:Eiheiji, Fukui|Eiheiji, Fukui]] in the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1977 in the case of the Tegurigajoyama Kofun, the largest of the group. Protection was extended to the remainder of the group in July 2005.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210110 |title=松岡古墳群 手繰ヶ城山古墳 石舟山古墳 鳥越山古墳 二本松山古墳 |trans-title= Matsuoka kofun gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The ''kofun'' group is located on a hillside with an elevation of 160 meters, overlooking the [[:en:Kuzuryū River|Kuzuryū River]] and the Echizen Plain. The cluster consists of about 150 tumuli, which are a mixture of square tumuli, circular tumuli, and keyhole-shaped tumuli. Among these tumuli, the four large keyhole-shape tumuli, ({{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above), which are estimated to date from the mid 4th to the late 5th century AD are covered under the National Historic Site designation. The tombs do not appear in any historical records and the name of Rank of the persons buried within are unknown, although per local legend, these were the graves of the "Kings of [[:en:Koshi Province (Japan)|Koshi]]" (越の国), the pre-[[:en:Imperial House of Japan|imperial dynasty]] state which controlled present-day Fukui, [[:en:Ishikawa Prefecture|Ishikawa]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama]] and [[:en:Niigata Prefecture|Niigata Prefecture]]s. [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] ((reigned 507 – 531 AD)) is said in some semi-legendary historical records to have originally been the King of Koshi, and therefore that the ''kofun'' in this area are the tombs for members of his clan.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
It is about a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Shiizakai Station|Shiizakai Station]] on the [[:en:Echizen Railway|Echizen Railway]] [[:en:Katsuyama Eiheiji Line|Katsuyama Eiheiji Line]] to the Tegurigajoyama Kofun.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==The kofun== {{nihongo|'''Tegurigajoyama Kofun'''|手繰ヶ城山古墳|}} is the largest of the four tumuli, with a total length of 128.4 meters, making it is the second largest found in the Hokuriku region of Japan after the [[Rokuroseyama Kofun Cluster|Rokuroseiyama Kofun]]. The circular portion is 78 meters in diameter and 19.7 meters high; the rectangular portion is in two tiers, with a length of 50.4 meters, width of 56.5 meters and a height of 15.2 meters. The tumulus is orientated to the northeast. It has a peculiar morphology in which the front part is extremely widened like a drumstick. Large numbers of ''[[fukiishi]]'' and cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' were found in the area. The details of the interior are unknown as the ''kofun'' has not been [[archaeological excavation|excavated]]; however, [[:en:Ground-penetrating radar|Ground-penetrating radar]] has confirmed that a stone-lined [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] exists near the apex of the posterior circle. The tumulus was dated to the middle of the fourth century AD.
{{nihongo|'''Torigoeyama Kofun'''|鳥越山古墳|}} is the smallest of the four tumuli, with a total length of 53.7 meters; however, it was found to have a boat-shaped stone [[:en:Sarcophagus|Sarcophagus]] and two horizontal passage graves. A large number of [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] were also uncovered, including stone spearheads, [[:en:Sharpening stone|whetstone]]s, fragments of iron implements, [[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] and horse fittings. The tumulus was partly carved out of the ground, and has no tiers. ''Haniwa'' have been found at the top of the posterior circle. It was dated to the middle of the fifth century.
{{nihongo|'''Ishifuneyama Kofun'''|石舟山古墳|}} is also a keyhole-shaped tumulus, and has a total length of 79.1 meters, with a secondary tumulus on its southwest side. The main tumulus has two-tiers, and ''haniwa'' have been found. It also dates from the middle of the fifth century. It was robbed at some unknown time, and fragments of a boat-shaped stone sarcophagus were exposed, thus leading to its name.
{{nihongo|'''Nihonmatsuyama Kofun'''|二本松山古墳|}} is a dome-shaped tumulus with a total length of 89 meters, although there is considerable controversy as to its structure, and prevalent option is that it is actually a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped tumulus]] which has partly collapsed. Neither [[fukiishi" nor ''haniwa'' have been found. It is built facing southwest on the summit of Nihonmatsuyama at an elevation of 273 meters on the south bank of the point where the Kuzuryu River enters the Fukui Plain. It was excavated in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1716–1736) at which time records indicate that armor and human remains were found and reburied. However, when was extensively excavated in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] only a number of grave goods were recovered, including [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s, fragments of a crown, cylindrical beads, swords and other fragments of weapons and a boat-shaped sarcophagus, but no armor or bones. The tumulus dates to the late fifth century. According to legend, this tumulus is the grave of Furuhime, the mother of the semi-legendary Emperor Keitai.
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukui)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukui)]]

The cluster consists of about 150 tumuli, which are a mixture of square tumuli, circular tumuli, and keyhole-shaped tumuli. Among these tumuli, the four large keyhole-shape tumuli, ({{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above), which are estimated to date from the mid 4th to the late 5th century AD are covered under the National Historic Site designation. The tombs do not appear in any historical records and the name of Rank of the persons buried within are unknown, although per local legend, these were the graves of the "Kings of [[:en:Koshi Province (Japan)|Koshi]]" (越の国), the pre-[[:en:Imperial House of Japan|imperial dynasty]] state which controlled present-day Fukui, [[:en:Ishikawa Prefecture|Ishikawa]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama]] and [[:en:Niigata Prefecture|Niigata Prefecture]]s. [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] ((reigned 507 – 531 AD)) is said in some semi-legendary historical records to have originally been the King of Koshi, and therefore that the ''kofun'' in this area are the tombs for members of his clan.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
It is about a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Shiizakai Station|Shiizakai Station]] on the [[:en:Echizen Railway|Echizen Railway]] [[:en:Katsuyama Eiheiji Line|Katsuyama Eiheiji Line]] to the Tegurigajoyama Kofun.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==The kofun== {{nihongo|'''Tegurigajoyama Kofun'''|手繰ヶ城山古墳|}} is the largest of the four tumuli, with a total length of 128.4 meters, making it is the second largest found in the Hokuriku region of Japan after the [[Rokuroseyama Kofun Cluster|Rokuroseiyama Kofun]]. The circular portion is 78 meters in diameter and 19.7 meters high; the rectangular portion is in two tiers, with a length of 50.4 meters, width of 56.5 meters and a height of 15.2 meters. The tumulus is orientated to the northeast. It has a peculiar morphology in which the front part is extremely widened like a drumstick. Large numbers of ''[[fukiishi]]'' and cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' were found in the area. The details of the interior are unknown as the ''kofun'' has not been [[archaeological excavation|excavated]]; however, [[:en:Ground-penetrating radar|Ground-penetrating radar]] has confirmed that a stone-lined [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] exists near the apex of the posterior circle. The tumulus was dated to the middle of the fourth century AD.
{{nihongo|'''Torigoeyama Kofun'''|鳥越山古墳|}} is the smallest of the four tumuli, with a total length of 53.7 meters; however, it was found to have a boat-shaped stone [[:en:Sarcophagus|Sarcophagus]] and two horizontal passage graves. A large number of [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] were also uncovered, including stone spearheads, [[:en:Sharpening stone|whetstone]]s, fragments of iron implements, [[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] and horse fittings. The tumulus was partly carved out of the ground, and has no tiers. ''Haniwa'' have been found at the top of the posterior circle. It was dated to the middle of the fifth century.
{{nihongo|'''Ishifuneyama Kofun'''|石舟山古墳|}} is also a keyhole-shaped tumulus, and has a total length of 79.1 meters, with a secondary tumulus on its southwest side. The main tumulus has two-tiers, and ''haniwa'' have been found. It also dates from the middle of the fifth century. It was robbed at some unknown time, and fragments of a boat-shaped stone sarcophagus were exposed, thus leading to its name.
{{nihongo|'''Nihonmatsuyama Kofun'''|二本松山古墳|}} is a dome-shaped tumulus with a total length of 89 meters, although there is considerable controversy as to its structure, and prevalent option is that it is actually a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped tumulus]] which has partly collapsed. Neither [[fukiishi" nor ''haniwa'' have been found. It is built facing southwest on the summit of Nihonmatsuyama at an elevation of 273 meters on the south bank of the point where the Kuzuryu River enters the Fukui Plain. It was excavated in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1716–1736) at which time records indicate that armor and human remains were found and reburied. However, when was extensively excavated in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] only a number of grave goods were recovered, including [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s, fragments of a crown, cylindrical beads, swords and other fragments of weapons and a boat-shaped sarcophagus, but no armor or bones. The tumulus dates to the late fifth century. According to legend, this tumulus is the grave of Furuhime, the mother of the semi-legendary Emperor Keitai.
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukui)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukui)]]

File:Tegurigajoyama Kofun.jpg| image_size = | alt = | caption = Tegurigajoyama Kofun | map_type = Japan Fukui Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = Matsuoka Kofun | relief = 1 | map_caption = Matsuoka Kofun cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates ={{coord|36|05|35|N|136|18|53.2|E|display=inline}}; {{Coord|36.093056|N|136.314778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}| map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Eiheiji, Fukui|Eiheiji, Fukui]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] | type = [[kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = late 4th or early 5th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Special Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Matsuoka Kofun Cluster'''|松岡古墳群|Matsuoka Kofun-gun}} is a group ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]]s located in what is now part of the town of [[:en:Eiheiji, Fukui|Eiheiji, Fukui]] in the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1977 in the case of the Tegurigajoyama Kofun, the largest of the group. Protection was extended to the remainder of the group in July 2005.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210110 |title=松岡古墳群 手繰ヶ城山古墳 石舟山古墳 鳥越山古墳 二本松山古墳 |trans-title= Matsuoka kofun gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The ''kofun'' group is located on a hillside with an elevation of 160 meters, overlooking the [[:en:Kuzuryū River|Kuzuryū River]] and the Echizen Plain. The cluster consists of about 150 tumuli, which are a mixture of square tumuli, circular tumuli, and keyhole-shaped tumuli. Among these tumuli, the four large keyhole-shape tumuli, ({{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above), which are estimated to date from the mid 4th to the late 5th century AD are covered under the National Historic Site designation. The tombs do not appear in any historical records and the name of Rank of the persons buried within are unknown, although per local legend, these were the graves of the "Kings of [[:en:Koshi Province (Japan)|Koshi]]" (越の国), the pre-[[:en:Imperial House of Japan|imperial dynasty]] state which controlled present-day Fukui, [[:en:Ishikawa Prefecture|Ishikawa]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama]] and [[:en:Niigata Prefecture|Niigata Prefecture]]s. [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] ((reigned 507 – 531 AD)) is said in some semi-legendary historical records to have originally been the King of Koshi, and therefore that the ''kofun'' in this area are the tombs for members of his clan.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
It is about a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Shiizakai Station|Shiizakai Station]] on the [[:en:Echizen Railway|Echizen Railway]] [[:en:Katsuyama Eiheiji Line|Katsuyama Eiheiji Line]] to the Tegurigajoyama Kofun.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==The kofun== {{nihongo|'''Tegurigajoyama Kofun'''|手繰ヶ城山古墳|}} is the largest of the four tumuli, with a total length of 128.4 meters, making it is the second largest found in the Hokuriku region of Japan after the [[Rokuroseyama Kofun Cluster|Rokuroseiyama Kofun]]. The circular portion is 78 meters in diameter and 19.7 meters high; the rectangular portion is in two tiers, with a length of 50.4 meters, width of 56.5 meters and a height of 15.2 meters. The tumulus is orientated to the northeast. It has a peculiar morphology in which the front part is extremely widened like a drumstick. Large numbers of ''[[fukiishi]]'' and cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' were found in the area. The details of the interior are unknown as the ''kofun'' has not been [[archaeological excavation|excavated]]; however, [[:en:Ground-penetrating radar|Ground-penetrating radar]] has confirmed that a stone-lined [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] exists near the apex of the posterior circle. The tumulus was dated to the middle of the fourth century AD.
{{nihongo|'''Torigoeyama Kofun'''|鳥越山古墳|}} is the smallest of the four tumuli, with a total length of 53.7 meters; however, it was found to have a boat-shaped stone [[:en:Sarcophagus|Sarcophagus]] and two horizontal passage graves. A large number of [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] were also uncovered, including stone spearheads, [[:en:Sharpening stone|whetstone]]s, fragments of iron implements, [[:en:Earthenware|Earthenware]] and horse fittings. The tumulus was partly carved out of the ground, and has no tiers. ''Haniwa'' have been found at the top of the posterior circle. It was dated to the middle of the fifth century.
{{nihongo|'''Ishifuneyama Kofun'''|石舟山古墳|}} is also a keyhole-shaped tumulus, and has a total length of 79.1 meters, with a secondary tumulus on its southwest side. The main tumulus has two-tiers, and ''haniwa'' have been found. It also dates from the middle of the fifth century. It was robbed at some unknown time, and fragments of a boat-shaped stone sarcophagus were exposed, thus leading to its name.
{{nihongo|'''Nihonmatsuyama Kofun'''|二本松山古墳|}} is a dome-shaped tumulus with a total length of 89 meters, although there is considerable controversy as to its structure, and prevalent option is that it is actually a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped tumulus]] which has partly collapsed. Neither [[fukiishi" nor ''haniwa'' have been found. It is built facing southwest on the summit of Nihonmatsuyama at an elevation of 273 meters on the south bank of the point where the Kuzuryu River enters the Fukui Plain. It was excavated in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] (1716–1736) at which time records indicate that armor and human remains were found and reburied. However, when was extensively excavated in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] only a number of grave goods were recovered, including [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]]s, fragments of a crown, cylindrical beads, swords and other fragments of weapons and a boat-shaped sarcophagus, but no armor or bones. The tumulus dates to the late fifth century. According to legend, this tumulus is the grave of Furuhime, the mother of the semi-legendary Emperor Keitai.
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukui)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Fukui)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:kk]][[Category:Matsuoka Kofun}} *[http://info.pref.fukui.jp/bunka/bunkazai/sitei/siseki/eiheijicho-matsuoka.html Cultural Heritage of Fukui Prefecture] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://kankou.town.eiheiji.lg.jp/guide/index.php?pathinfo=kofungun Eiheiji town guide] {{in lang|ja}}

Among these tumuli, the four large keyhole-shape tumuli, ({{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|comement=second one attempting to fix this error|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which are shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above), which are estimated to date from the mid 4th to the late 5th century AD are covered under the National Historic Site designation. The tombs do not appear in any historical records and the name of Rank of the persons buried within are unknown, although per local legend, these were the graves of the "Kings of {{ill|Koshi Province (Japan)|en|Koshi Province (Japan)|WD=Q1351611|lt=Koshi}}" (越の国), the pre-{{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=imperial dynasty}} state which controlled present-day Fukui, {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|en|Ishikawa Prefecture|WD=Q131281|lt=Ishikawa}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|WD=Q132929|lt=Toyama}} and {{ill|Niigata Prefecture|en|Niigata Prefecture|WD=Q132705|lt=Niigata Prefecture}}s. {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}} ((reigned 507 – 531 AD)) is said in some semi-legendary historical records to have originally been the King of Koshi, and therefore that the ''kofun'' in this area are the tombs for members of his clan.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
It is about a 20-minute walk from {{ill|Shiizakai Station|en|Shiizakai Station|WD=Q7496501|lt=Shiizakai Station}} on the {{ill|Echizen Railway|en|Echizen Railway|WD=Q1007890|lt=Echizen Railway}} {{ill|Katsuyama Eiheiji Line|en|Katsuyama Eiheiji Line|WD=Q6378238|lt=Katsuyama Eiheiji Line}} to the Tegurigajoyama Kofun.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==The kofun== {{nihongo|'''Tegurigajoyama Kofun'''|手繰ヶ城山古墳|}} is the largest of the four tumuli, with a total length of 128.4 meters, making it is the second largest found in the Hokuriku region of Japan after the [[Rokuroseyama Kofun Cluster|Rokuroseiyama Kofun]]. The circular portion is 78 meters in diameter and 19.7 meters high; the rectangular portion is in two tiers, with a length of 50.4 meters, width of 56.5 meters and a height of 15.2 meters. The tumulus is orientated to the northeast. It has a peculiar morphology in which the front part is extremely widened like a drumstick. Large numbers of ''[[fukiishi]]'' and cylindrical ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}}'' were found in the area. The details of the interior are unknown as the ''kofun'' has not been [[archaeological excavation|excavated]]; however, {{ill|Ground-penetrating radar|en|Ground-penetrating radar|WD=Q503560|lt=Ground-penetrating radar}} has confirmed that a stone-lined [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] exists near the apex of the posterior circle. The tumulus was dated to the middle of the fourth century AD.
{{nihongo|'''Torigoeyama Kofun'''|鳥越山古墳|}} is the smallest of the four tumuli, with a total length of 53.7 meters; however, it was found to have a boat-shaped stone {{ill|Sarcophagus|en|Sarcophagus|WD=Q48634|lt=Sarcophagus}} and two horizontal passage graves. A large number of {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}} were also uncovered, including stone spearheads, {{ill|Sharpening stone|en|Sharpening stone|WD=Q507559|lt=whetstone}}s, fragments of iron implements, {{ill|Earthenware|en|Earthenware|WD=Q1377111|lt=Earthenware}} and horse fittings. The tumulus was partly carved out of the ground, and has no tiers. ''Haniwa'' have been found at the top of the posterior circle. It was dated to the middle of the fifth century.
{{nihongo|'''Ishifuneyama Kofun'''|石舟山古墳|}} is also a keyhole-shaped tumulus, and has a total length of 79.1 meters, with a secondary tumulus on its southwest side. The main tumulus has two-tiers, and ''haniwa'' have been found. It also dates from the middle of the fifth century. It was robbed at some unknown time, and fragments of a boat-shaped stone sarcophagus were exposed, thus leading to its name.
{{nihongo|'''Nihonmatsuyama Kofun'''|二本松山古墳|}} is a dome-shaped tumulus with a total length of 89 meters, although there is considerable controversy as to its structure, and prevalent option is that it is actually a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=keyhole-shaped tumulus}} which has partly collapsed. Neither [[fukiishi" nor ''haniwa'' have been found. It is built facing southwest on the summit of Nihonmatsuyama at an elevation of 273 meters on the south bank of the point where the Kuzuryu River enters the Fukui Plain. It was excavated in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} (1716–1736) at which time records indicate that armor and human remains were found and reburied. However, when was extensively excavated in the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} only a number of grave goods were recovered, including {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|WD=Q1041984|lt=Bronze mirror}}s, fragments of a crown, cylindrical beads, swords and other fragments of weapons and a boat-shaped sarcophagus, but no armor or bones. The tumulus dates to the late fifth century. According to legend, this tumulus is the grave of Furuhime, the mother of the semi-legendary Emperor Keitai.
